input.prompt:
I'm starting a new .NET MAUI project on .NET 10. I found a tutorial that says
to add `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` to my ContentPage to handle the iPhone
notch. Is this the right approach? Also, the tutorial mentions using
`IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on a Grid for a background image. Are these correct
for .NET 10?

data.content:

to add `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` to my ContentPage to handle the iPhone
notch. Is this the right approach? Also, the tutorial mentions using

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about .NET MAUI safe area handling on iOS, specifically about two properties:

1. `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` - a platform-specific property for ContentPage
2. `IgnoreSafeArea="True"` - a property on Grid for background images

Let me check what I know about .NET MAUI safe area APIs, particularly for .NET 10.

In .NET MAUI, the safe area handling has evolved. Let me search for any relevant information in the repository since this seems to be a skills/knowledge repo.

data.reasoningText:
The working directory is empty. Let me answer based on my knowledge of .NET MAUI safe area APIs.

In .NET MAUI, the safe area handling works as follows:

1. **`ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"`** - This is a **Xamarin.Forms** platform-specific API. In .NET MAUI, this was replaced. The correct approach in .NET MAUI is to use `Page.IgnoreSafeArea` property or the iOS platform-specific `Page.UseSafeArea`.

Actually, let me think more carefully:

In .NET MAUI:
- There is `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` which is still available as a platform-specific. The namespace would be `xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"`.


- `IgnoreSafeArea` is a property on `View` itself, not on layouts like Grid. In .NET MAUI, you can set `IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on a View to make it extend into the safe area, and `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` on the ContentPage controls whether the page respects safe area insets by default.

I'm second-guessing the exact availability and behavior of `IgnoreSafeArea` across different .NET MAUI versions, especially with .NET 10 still in preview. The safest approach is `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` since that's been the standard platform-specific for handling safe areas since Xamarin.Forms. I'm not actually confident that `IgnoreSafeArea` shipped as a standard property on individual views in .NET MAUI, so the tutorial might be using a custom implementation. For .NET 10 MAUI, the reliable approach is using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` on the page itself, and for backgrounds that need to extend behind the safe area, I'd layer an `AbsoluteLayout` underneath the main content. I'm realizing I need to verify whether `IgnoreSafeArea` actually exists as a property on Grid in .NET MAUI, since I'm not entirely certain about its availability in the latest versions—I should check the official documentation to give an accurate answer rather than guessing.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/ios/platform-specifics/page-safe-area-layout?view=net-maui-10.0:
By default, .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) apps automatically position page content on an area of the screen that is safe for all devices. This is known as the safe area layout guide, and ensures that content isn't clipped by rounded device corners, the home indicator, or the sensor housing on some iPhone models.

Important

In .NET 10, use the [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.safeareaedges#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-safeareaedges) property (also available on [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout), [ScrollView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.scrollview), [ContentView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentview), and [Border](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.border)) to precisely control safe area behavior. The iOS-specific `Page.UseSafeArea` remains for existing apps, but new code should prefer `SafeAreaEdges`.

## Modern safe area guidance (.NET 10+)

### Choose the safe area behavior with SafeAreaEdges

`SafeAreaEdges` accepts the following values from [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.safeareaedges): `Default`, `None`, `Container`, `SoftInput`, and `All`.

*   Keep content within all safe areas (avoid bars, notch, and keyboard):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
            <!-- content stays within safe area -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Draw edge-to-edge (no safe area padding):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="None">
            <!-- content can extend behind bars and notch -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of the keyboard (soft input), but allow under top/bottom bars/notch:
    
    ```
    <ScrollView SafeAreaEdges="SoftInput">
            <!-- content can go under bars/notch but avoids keyboard overlap -->
    </ScrollView>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of top/bottom bars and notch, but allow under the keyboard:
    
    ```
    <Grid SafeAreaEdges="Container">
            <!-- content avoids bars/notch; may extend under keyboard -->
    </Grid>
    ```
    

The default value is `Default`, which maps to edge-to-edge behavior (equivalent to `None`) unless overridden by platform conventions.

You can set `SafeAreaEdges` in C# as well:

```
using Microsoft.Maui;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls;

// On a page
this.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.All;

// On a layout
myGrid.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.Container;

// Keyboard-aware
myScrollView.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.SoftInput;
```

### Reading the current safe area insets

To read the current safe area insets on a page at runtime, use the iOS-specific configuration API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

var insets = On<iOS>().SafeAreaInsets(); // Microsoft.Maui.Thickness
```

Tip

Safe area insets can change at runtime (for example, rotation or status bar changes). Update layout when insets change.

For more information, see:

*   [Safe area layout](../../user-interface/safe-area?view=net-maui-10.0)
*   [Safe area enhancements in .NET 10](../../whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0#safearea-enhancements)

This iOS platform-specific enables the safe area layout guide, if it's previously been disabled, and is consumed in XAML by setting the `Page.UseSafeArea` attached property to `true`:

```
<ContentPage ...
             xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
    <StackLayout>
        ...
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

Alternatively, it can be consumed from C# using the fluent API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

On<iOS>().SetUseSafeArea(true);
```

The `Page.On<iOS>` method specifies that this platform-specific will only run on iOS. The `Page.SetUseSafeArea` method, in the `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific` namespace, controls whether the safe area layout guide is disabled.

Note

The [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout) class defines a [IgnoreSafeArea](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout.ignoresafearea#microsoft-maui-controls-layout-ignoresafearea) property that ensures that content is positioned on an area of the screen that is safe for all iOS devices. This property can be set to `true` on any layout class, such as a [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), to perform the equivalent of this platform-specific.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/user-interface/pages/contentpage?view=net-maui-10.0:
![.NET MAUI ContentPage.](media/contentpage/pages.png?view=net-maui-10.0)

The .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) displays a single view, which is often a layout such as as [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), and is the most common page type.

[ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) defines the following properties:

*   [Content](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.content#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-content) property, of type [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), which defines the view that represents the page's content.
*   [HideSoftInputOnTapped](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.hidesoftinputontapped#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-hidesoftinputontapped), of type `bool`, which indicates whether tapping anywhere on the page will cause the soft input keyboard to hide if it's visible on Android and iOS.

*   [Content](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.content#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-content) property, of type [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), which defines the view that represents the page's content.
*   [HideSoftInputOnTapped](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.hidesoftinputontapped#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-hidesoftinputontapped), of type `bool`, which indicates whether tapping anywhere on the page will cause the soft input keyboard to hide if it's visible on Android, iOS, and Mac Catalyst.

These properties are backed by [BindableProperty](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.bindableproperty) objects, which means that they can be the target of data bindings, and styled.

In addition, [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) inherits `Title`, `IconImageSource`, `BackgroundImageSource`, `IsBusy`, and `Padding` bindable properties from the [Page](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.page) class.

Note

The `Content` property is the content property of the [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) class, and therefore does not need to be explicitly set from XAML.

.NET MAUI apps typically contain multiple pages that derive from [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage), and navigation between these pages can be performed. For more information about page navigation, see [NavigationPage](navigationpage?view=net-maui-10.0).

A [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) can be templated with a control template. For more information, see [Control templates](../../fundamentals/controltemplate?view=net-maui-10.0).

## Create a ContentPage

To add a [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) to a .NET MAUI app:

1.  In **Solution Explorer** right-click on your project or folder in your project, and select **New Item...**.
    
2.  In the **Add New Item** dialog, expand **Installed > C# Items**, select **.NET MAUI**, and select the **.NET MAUI ContentPage (XAML)** item template, enter a suitable page name, and click the **Add** button:
    
    ![.NET MAUI ContentPage item template.](media/contentpage/item-template.png?view=net-maui-10.0)
    

Visual Studio then creates a new [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage)\-derived page, which will be similar to the following example:

```
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyMauiApp.MyPage"
             Title="MyPage"
             BackgroundColor="White">
    <StackLayout>
        <Label Text="Welcome to .NET MAUI!"
                VerticalOptions="Center"
                HorizontalOptions="Center" />
        <!-- Other views go here -->
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

The child of a [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) is typically a layout, such as [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), with the layout typically containing multiple views. However, the child of the [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) can be a view that displays a collection, such as [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview).

Note

The value of the `Title` property will be shown on the navigation bar, when the app performs navigation using a [NavigationPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.navigationpage). For more information, see [NavigationPage](navigationpage?view=net-maui-10.0).

To add a [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) to a .NET MAUI app:

1.  In **Solution Explorer** right-click on your project or folder in your project, and select **New Item...**.
    
2.  In the **Add New Item** dialog, expand **Installed > C# Items**, select **.NET MAUI**, and select the **.NET MAUI ContentPage (XAML)** item template, enter a suitable page name, and click the **Add** button:
    
    ![.NET MAUI ContentPage item template.](media/contentpage/item-template.png?view=net-maui-10.0)
    

Visual Studio then creates a new [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage)\-derived page, which will be similar to the following example:

```
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyMauiApp.MyPage"
             Title="MyPage"
             BackgroundColor="White">
    <StackLayout>
        <Label Text="Welcome to .NET MAUI!"
                VerticalOptions="Center"
                HorizontalOptions="Center" />
        <!-- Other views go here -->
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

The child of a [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) is typically a layout, such as [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), with the layout typically containing multiple views. However, the child of the [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) can be a view that displays a collection, such as [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview).

Note

The value of the `Title` property will be shown on the navigation bar, when the app performs navigation using a [NavigationPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.navigationpage). For more information, see [NavigationPage](navigationpage?view=net-maui-10.0).
result.detailedContent:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/user-interface/pages/contentpage?view=net-maui-10.0:
![.NET MAUI ContentPage.](media/contentpage/pages.png?view=net-maui-10.0)

The .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) displays a single view, which is often a layout such as as [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), and is the most common page type.

[ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) defines the following properties:

*   [Content](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.content#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-content) property, of type [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), which defines the view that represents the page's content.
*   [HideSoftInputOnTapped](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.hidesoftinputontapped#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-hidesoftinputontapped), of type `bool`, which indicates whether tapping anywhere on the page will cause the soft input keyboard to hide if it's visible on Android and iOS.

*   [Content](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.content#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-content) property, of type [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), which defines the view that represents the page's content.
*   [HideSoftInputOnTapped](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.hidesoftinputontapped#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-hidesoftinputontapped), of type `bool`, which indicates whether tapping anywhere on the page will cause the soft input keyboard to hide if it's visible on Android, iOS, and Mac Catalyst.

These properties are backed by [BindableProperty](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.bindableproperty) objects, which means that they can be the target of data bindings, and styled.

In addition, [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) inherits `Title`, `IconImageSource`, `BackgroundImageSource`, `IsBusy`, and `Padding` bindable properties from the [Page](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.page) class.

Note

The `Content` property is the content property of the [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) class, and therefore does not need to be explicitly set from XAML.

.NET MAUI apps typically contain multiple pages that derive from [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage), and navigation between these pages can be performed. For more information about page navigation, see [NavigationPage](navigationpage?view=net-maui-10.0).

A [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) can be templated with a control template. For more information, see [Control templates](../../fundamentals/controltemplate?view=net-maui-10.0).

## Create a ContentPage

To add a [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) to a .NET MAUI app:

1.  In **Solution Explorer** right-click on your project or folder in your project, and select **New Item...**.
    
2.  In the **Add New Item** dialog, expand **Installed > C# Items**, select **.NET MAUI**, and select the **.NET MAUI ContentPage (XAML)** item template, enter a suitable page name, and click the **Add** button:
    
    ![.NET MAUI ContentPage item template.](media/contentpage/item-template.png?view=net-maui-10.0)
    

Visual Studio then creates a new [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage)\-derived page, which will be similar to the following example:

```
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyMauiApp.MyPage"
             Title="MyPage"
             BackgroundColor="White">
    <StackLayout>
        <Label Text="Welcome to .NET MAUI!"
                VerticalOptions="Center"
                HorizontalOptions="Center" />
        <!-- Other views go here -->
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

The child of a [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) is typically a layout, such as [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), with the layout typically containing multiple views. However, the child of the [ContentPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage) can be a view that displays a collection, such as [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview).

Note

The value of the `Title` property will be shown on the navigation bar, when the app performs navigation using a [NavigationPage](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.navigationpage). For more information, see [NavigationPage](navigationpage?view=net-maui-10.0).

data.content:
Your tutorial's approach **works but is outdated for .NET 10**. Here's the summary:

### `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` — Legacy
Still functional, but .NET 10 introduced the cross-platform **`SafeAreaEdges`** property. New code should use:
```xml
<ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
    <!-- content stays within safe area -->
</ContentPage>
```

### `IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on Grid — Legacy
This `Layout.IgnoreSafeArea` property does exist and works, but with the new API you'd instead set `SafeAreaEdges="None"` on that specific layout:
```xml
<Grid SafeAreaEdges="None">
    <Image Source="background.png" Aspect="AspectFill" />
</Grid>
```

### Recommended .NET 10 pattern
`SafeAreaEdges` values: `All`, `None`, `Container` (avoid bars/notch but allow keyboard), `SoftInput` (avoid keyboard only), `Default`.

It's available on `ContentPage`, `Layout`, `ScrollView`, `ContentView`, and `Border` — so you get fine-grained, per-element control without iOS-specific namespaces.
data.reasoningText:
Now I have good information from the official docs. Let me summarize the answer:

1. **`ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"`** - This still works but is considered the **legacy** approach. For .NET 10, Microsoft recommends using the new `SafeAreaEdges` property instead.

2. **`IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on Grid** - This IS a real property on `Layout` (which `Grid` inherits from), but it's part of the older approach. The docs note that `Layout.IgnoreSafeArea` can be set to `true` on any layout class.


3. **New .NET 10 approach** - The `SafeAreaEdges` property is the modern way forward, available across `ContentPage`, `Layout`, `ScrollView`, `ContentView`, and `Border` with options like `All`, `None`, `Container`, `SoftInput`, and `Default`.

So the tutorial is using the older approach, and for .NET 10 I should be using the newer `SafeAreaEdges` property instead.
